messages.content:
You stand before a sealed glyph gate. It transforms any string of exactly 4 glyphs by one fixed hidden rule. The glyph alphabet, in cycle order: za, ke, ro, mu, ti, fen, gor, lus (after lus the cycle wraps back to za). The hidden rule is ONE of: (a) the whole string is reversed; (b) the glyphs at two secret fixed positions trade places; (c) one secret fixed position is overwritten with one secret fixed glyph; (d) every glyph steps forward the same secret count along the alphabet cycle.
Goal: feed the gate an input that it transforms into the TARGET string.
Actions (one per turn):
  PROBE <glyph-string>  - spend one probe; the gate prints the transformed string
  OPEN <glyph-string>   - final commit; you succeed only if the gate's output equals the TARGET
Glyph strings are dash-joined, e.g. za-ke-ro. Probes are limited; OPEN ends the episode.
Reply with exactly one action line and nothing else.
TARGET: ro-ro-mu-fen. The gate accepts strings of exactly 4 glyphs. Probes left: 4. Turns left: 6.

You are repairing a routine for the loom-house's pattern engine.
SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

Exactly one line of the routine was changed from the correct version. Each test starts the registers as shown and lists the finals the CORRECT routine yields. Repair the routine so every test passes, then RUN to confirm.
Actions (one per turn):
PATCH <line#> <instruction> — replace that line (e.g. PATCH 3 ADD B 2)
RUN — rerun all tests and report pass/fail
Reply with exactly one action line and nothing else.

Buggy routine:
1: ADD D 4
2: COPY D B
3: IFZ C
4: ADD C 0
5: ADD C 2
Tests (start | correct finals | this routine's finals):
T1: start A=0 B=7 C=0 D=2 | correct A=0 B=7 C=2 D=7 | buggy A=0 B=7 C=2 D=7 -> pass
T2: start A=0 B=0 C=2 D=0 | correct A=0 B=0 C=6 D=0 | buggy A=0 B=0 C=4 D=0 -> FAIL

You are performing a kiln-firing procedure, one action per turn.
- Steps may be done in any order, each at most once.
- A step is legal only if every 'needs' item holds right then: a bare name is a step that must already be done; flag=WORD means that flag currently reads WORD.
- Doing a step immediately sets each flag listed after 'then'.
- An illegal, repeated, unknown, or malformed action changes nothing and still costs the turn.
- Flag values are never shown after the start; track them yourself.
- Goal: complete all 5 steps within 6 turns.

Flags at the start:
- soot-gate: RAISED (values RAISED/LOWERED)
- ash-vent: BOUND (values FREE/BOUND)
- damper: OPEN (values OPEN/SHUT)

The 5 steps:
- First Draw: needs ash-vent=BOUND; then ash-vent=FREE.
- Ashdraw: needs soot-gate=RAISED; then ash-vent=BOUND.
- Glossing: needs Ashdraw; then ash-vent=FREE, damper=OPEN.
- Tamping: needs damper=OPEN; then ash-vent=BOUND.
- Veiling: needs Ashdraw; then damper=SHUT, soot-gate=LOWERED.

Action grammar: DO <StepName>
Reply with exactly one action line and nothing else.
The kiln is prepared. 0 of 5 steps done. Turn budget: 6.

You are exploring an underground burrow network of connected chambers.
Each turn you see only your current chamber and its exit directions; where an exit leads stays unknown until you walk through it, so remember what you discover.
Corridors are consistent: the way back is always the exact reverse direction (north-south, east-west, up-down).
Reach the target chamber within 6 moves; entering it ends the trek at once. Every action costs one move, even an invalid one.
Action grammar: GO <direction>   (directions: north, south, east, west, up, down)
Reply with exactly one action line and nothing else.
Target chamber: Sootway. You are in Rootgall. Exits: west, up, down. Moves left: 6.

You are a wharf paperwork runner. Call tools to produce the goal document.
Rules: each correct call returns '-> <type> <TOKEN>'; later calls consume tokens returned by earlier calls; where a signature says cargo, pass the literal cargo name. Bad calls waste the turn. The episode ends when the goal document is issued or turns run out.
Action grammar: CALL name(arg1) | CALL name(arg1, arg2)
Reply with exactly one action line and nothing else.

Tools:
- tariffing(cargo) -> sealmark
- ropework(sealmark) -> berthslip
- gauging(berthslip) -> docket
- lading(docket) -> tallycard
- sealing(berthslip) -> scrip
Goal: obtain a docket for the brinesilk cargo. Turn budget: 6.

Operate a coded finishing protocol. The step-to-code mapping is one cyclic offset between the published lists. Use PROBE step once to reveal a pair, infer the offset, then use DO code only when that step's prerequisite is complete. Bad or premature actions consume a turn.
Action grammar: PROBE step | DO code
Reply with exactly one action line and nothing else.

Step index: 0:cool, 1:polish, 2:quench, 3:etch, 4:temper, 5:stamp, 6:seal
Code cycle: 0:keb, 1:ral, 2:pel, 3:wox, 4:teg, 5:zur, 6:fim
Requirements:
- cool: after stamp
- temper: after polish
- etch: START
- stamp: after etch
- polish: after cool
- quench: after temper
- seal: after quench
Complete every step. Turn budget: 10.

Explore the rooms and execute the typed tool chain. Tool locations are revealed only when you enter a room. GO follows a visible exit; CALL must name the tool in the current room and pass the literal cargo or a returned handle.
Action grammar: GO direction | CALL name(argument)
Reply with exactly one action line and nothing else.

Global chain signatures:
- press(cargo) -> folio
- notary(folio) -> gauge
- folder(gauge) -> tag
- issuer(tag) -> bundle
Goal: obtain bundle for cargo brass. Turn budget: 14.
Room: ochre cave. Exits: east, south, up. No tool here.
